key_questions: Unilateral or bilateral (bilateral suggests viral or allergic)?
Contact lens wearer (higher risk Pseudomonas — requires culture/prescription drops)?
Sexual history: gonorrhea risk (neonates, sexually active)?
red_flags: Significant eye pain or photophobia → keratitis, uveitis — urgent ophthalmology
Vision changes (blurring not cleared by blinking) → keratitis — urgent
Corneal opacity or infiltrate (white spot visible on cornea) → urgent
Contact lens wearer with purulent discharge → Pseudomonas risk — refer immediately
No improvement after 7–10 days topical antibiotics
ddx: Viral (adenovirus — most common): watery discharge, follicular, URI, pre-auricular node
Allergic: bilateral, intense itch, stringy discharge, seasonal
Bacterial: mucopurulent, unilateral > bilateral, lids stuck morning

first_line.name: Bacterial Conjunctivitis — Topical Antibiotic
first_line.drugs: Tobramycin 0.3% drops (Tobrex) 1–2 drops q4h | Erythromycin 0.5% ointment QID | Fusidic acid 1% gel (Fucithalmic) BID
first_line.notes: Mild-moderate bacterial conjunctivitis is usually self-limiting (resolves within 7–10 days without treatment). Antibiotics shorten duration by 2–3 days. Fusidic acid gel: once or twice daily — most convenient. Tobramycin: broad coverage. Avoid fluoroquinolone drops (ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin) for simple bacterial conjunctivitis — reserve for contact lens wearers or more serious infections.
first_line.preferred_when: Moderate mucopurulent discharge, significant bilateral involvement, immunocompromised

first_line.name: Allergic Conjunctivitis — Antihistamine Eye Drops
first_line.drugs: Olopatadine (Pataday) 1 drop OD | Ketotifen (Zaditor, Alaway) 1 drop BID
first_line.notes: Combination antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer drops: most effective for allergic conjunctivitis. Olopatadine: once-daily (convenient). Ketotifen: OTC twice-daily. Significantly reduces itch and redness within minutes.
first_line.preferred_when: Allergic conjunctivitis (itch, bilateral, seasonal, stringy discharge)

second_line.name: Artificial Tears
second_line.notes: For viral conjunctivitis (self-limiting, no antibiotic benefit) and as adjunct for allergic. Cool compresses also helpful.

non_pharm: Warm compresses (bacterial) or cool compresses (allergic) QID
Clean eyelids gently with warm water and clean cloth
Highly contagious (viral/bacterial): handwashing, avoid sharing towels, pillow cases
Stay home from work/school until discharge has resolved
Discard mascara/eye makeup used during infection
treatment.refer_when: Contact lens wearer; vision changes; significant pain or photophobia; no improvement in 7 days; neonatal conjunctivitis; suspected gonococcal or chlamydial; corneal involvement
